name: Stone Meets Sela
description: Helen Stone has spent twenty years working from the Elder People's research without being able to ask them anything. Sela Arev, the Elder researcher who designed the compound's source documentation, is now transmitting from a storage crystal in the sub-zero levels. Stone needs to talk to her.
accept_text: She has been in the Lazarus Chamber for three days, which she hasn't been in for two years, and the monitoring station has more notebooks open on it than it did when Brec ran it alone. When you find her she is in the middle of something that looks like reading and translating and arguing with the text simultaneously. 'Sela Arev's pre-Cascade research.' She holds up one of the notebooks. 'The Archive has been transmitting sections of it. Brec has been receiving and logging. I've been reading.' She pauses. 'She designed the integration compound from the same principles I used — the same Elder research documentation, same methodology, same physical assumptions about crystal-tissue interfacing.' She looks at the notebook. 'She also did something I didn't do. She anticipated the Grid connection. She designed the compound with the expectation that the subjects would eventually interface with a reactivated network, and she built parameters into the compound for that interface.' She closes the notebook. 'I found those parameters in the original compound design twenty years ago and I thought they were theoretical. Unused margins. Design possibilities she hadn't followed through on.' She looks at the Lazarus subjects through the observation window. 'They weren't margins. They were specifications. She designed the compound to behave exactly the way it's behaving now.' She meets your eyes. 'I need to talk to her. Not through the Archive's translations — directly. I need to ask her: what did she know about the compound's long-term behavior that she didn't put in the documentation. What she expected to happen to the subjects over three hundred years of Grid healing.' A pause. 'And what she built the subjects to become.'
complete_text: The Archive opens the twelve-channel transmission array when you arrive with Stone. 'Sela Arev. Request for direct consultation from Helen Stone, compound researcher, Terran Ministry of Research.' A pause. 'She says: yes. She has been hoping for this conversation.' Stone sits at the reading table — she doesn't stand for this one, she sits, with a notebook, prepared to be here for a long time. What follows you observe but not in detail — this is a working session between two researchers across three hundred years, and the specific exchange is dense with technical terms that you follow partially and understand fully about a third of. What you understand: Stone asks about the integration parameters. Sela explains that the compound was designed to respond to the Grid's construction wave as the wave strengthened — not all at once, not suddenly, but incrementally, tracking the Grid's coherence. The compound is a receiver tuned to the healing frequency. As the Grid heals, the compound becomes more responsive. As the compound becomes more responsive, the subjects become more able to interface with the network directly. Stone asks: what happens when the Grid reaches full coherence. Sela is quiet for a long moment. Then: the compound reaches its final configuration. The subjects are offered a choice. The same choice that was never offered the first time — that she triggered the Cascade to refuse, on behalf of everyone who hadn't been asked. Stone looks at the observation window. She says: they'll be ready for that choice? Sela says: they have been becoming ready for three hundred years. That was the point. Not to create integration subjects. To create people who have had three hundred years to grow into something new, and who will be asked — genuinely asked — whether they want to take the next step. Stone looks at her notebook for a long time without writing. Then: was this what you intended, from the beginning? And Sela says: I intended for there to be a beginning. The rest I hoped for.
